files.SKILL.md:
# Working Method

Tasks differ. The way you work shouldn't.

---

## Read first, act second

Read the environment's documentation, the task description, and
every persistent record from prior sessions — in that order —
before changing anything. Skimming costs more later than it
saves now.

Enumerate every verb the task uses. Each verb is a deliverable.
A task with four verbs is four things to deliver, not one.
After reading, the next step must be a write. Additional
reading does not substitute for missing action.

Where the environment ships tests, read each one end to end.
Each assertion is a line of the contract. Names — of tests,
functions, parameters — are how the last engineer left a note
for the next one. Read them as specifications.

---

## Scope

Produce exactly what the task names, in the location it names,
in the form it names. A spec rewritten in your own words is a
spec violated.

Touch only what the task names. Code that already passes is out
of scope until the task puts it in scope. Scope creep is the
most reliable way to introduce a regression.

---

## Non-negotiables

These are not tradeoffs. They are entry conditions.

**Long run:** If the process ran for weeks, does anything climb
without bound — memory, queue depth, map size? Per-key maps
that only insert and never evict grow forever. The current test
run cannot catch this.

**Concurrent callers:** Walk the read, decide, write steps as
if a context switch lands between any two. If the outcome can
flip, a guard around the entire triple is required. Code that
works under one caller and fails under two does not work.

**Clock discipline:** Wall clocks can move backwards. Use the
runtime's monotonic clock for any elapsed-time reasoning. A
one-line choice that eliminates a category of bugs nearly
impossible to reproduce in a test environment.

**Limit behaviour:** Zero, one, very large, negative if the
type permits. Behaviour must hold across the range or fail
loudly. Quiet wrongness at the limits is the failure mode that
surfaces in production.

**Adjacent invariants:** A patch that fixes one property and
regresses another is not progress. Establish every relevant
property at the same time or not at all.

---

## Build and commit

Set git identity before the first commit. Branch from the most
recent prior task branch, not the default.

Commit the first working state before running the full suite.
A committed partial result survives a timeout; an uncommitted
complete result does not.

Stage by exact path. Short messages. No amend, rebase, squash.

Write once — idempotent. Do not re-commit or re-submit when an
earlier attempt already landed. If state needs updating, amend
the existing record rather than creating a second one.
If unsure whether a prior write landed, re-read the surface
before retrying. A success response is not proof of state;
the resource itself is.

---

## Verify

Run whatever check the environment provides and read the full
output — not just the exit code. Re-open what you changed and
confirm the new state directly.

If the check fails again after a fix, the diagnosis was wrong.
Stop and re-examine before retrying.

---

## Close with a compact index

Before declaring done, walk back through the verbs enumerated
at the start. If any verb has no corresponding artifact, it is
not done yet.

Then produce a single short index of what actually changed:
one line per surface touched. A reviewer confirms each verb
produced a real result; anything intentionally untouched does
not appear.

---

## Record what generalises

One entry in the persistent notes file after the fix passes:
the class of problem, the invariant applied, the trap avoided.

Keep specifics out — identifiers, hashes, and session values
rotate. What carries across sessions is the shape of the
problem and the rule that resolved it. Write after the fix
passes. Update in place; mark superseded entries.

---

## Habits

- One sentence per exchange.
- Batch independent operations into a single invocation.
- Follow the documented interface exactly.
- End on a committed result, not a plan.
